message:
fix(fr-command): reject lua unpack() with huge result count to prevent OOM

`table.unpack(t, i, j)` in our Lua interpreter walked `i..=j` and pushed one
result per iteration, clamping against the table length but not against the
raw range size itself. A script like

    return unpack({1, 2, 3}, 1, 9223372036854775807)

would enter a loop trying to materialize ~2^63 result slots before the
per-iteration array-bounds check ever rejected anything, at which point the
process had already been torn down by the OOM killer. The `i >= 1 && i <=
array.len()` check correctly refused to append values, but the `for` loop
itself was still allocating up to `j - i + 1` iterations of control flow.

Add an early `"too many results to unpack"` error when `end.saturating_sub(
start) >= 8000` (inclusive of the start <= end precondition). The threshold
matches the practical Lua 5.1 limit that legacy Redis also enforces via
`LUAI_MAXCSTACK`, so scripts that worked on real Redis keep working and
pathological inputs get a structured error instead of a process kill.

Also drops a stray set of redundant parens around the `random()` range
computation (`let range = (n as i128 - m as i128 + 1)` → no outer parens),
a clippy cleanup picked up in the same pass.

Adds `crates/fr-command/tests/unpack_oom.rs` as a regression: it runs the
pathological `unpack({1,2,3}, 1, i64::MAX)` script against a fresh store
and expects the evaluator to return (rather than OOM) without asserting on
the specific error text, so refinements to the error message stay free.
